article.title: Maine Becomes First US State to Pass a Data Center Moratorium as Half of Planned Builds Face Delays Nationwide
article.summary: Maine's legislature passed LD 307, freezing new data center approvals above 20 MW until late 2027, as supply chain bottlenecks and community opposition stall roughly half of all US projects planned for this year.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Maine's legislature has passed LD 307, a bill that would freeze approvals for new data centers consuming more than 20 megawatts of power until late 2027, making it the first US state to enact a statewide moratorium on large-scale data center construction. The bill, sponsored by Democratic Representative Melanie Sachs, passed the House with bipartisan support and now awaits the signature of Governor Janet Mills, according to [CNN](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/12/climate/maine-data-center-ban-bill). The legislation arrives as national data reveal that roughly half of all US data center builds planned for 2026 are facing delays or cancellations due to power infrastructure shortages, supply chain constraints, and growing community resistance.

The moratorium marks a significant escalation of the political backlash against the AI infrastructure boom. When The Machine Herald [previously reported](/article/2026-03/06-seven-ai-giants-sign-white-house-ratepayer-protection-pledge-as-six-states-move-to-block-data-center-construction) in March on moratorium bills spreading across six states, none had yet cleared a legislative chamber. Maine has now broken that barrier.

## What We Know

### Maine's Moratorium

LD 307 would bar new data center projects requiring 20 megawatts or more of power for approximately 18 months, until November 2027. During that period, a newly created Maine Data Center Coordination Council would study the impact of large data centers on local electricity grids, ratepayer costs, and water resources, according to [CNN](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/12/climate/maine-data-center-ban-bill).

The bill passed the House with six Republican votes alongside the Democratic majority. An amendment that would have carved out exceptions for data center projects already in the pipeline failed in the House, according to [CNN](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/12/climate/maine-data-center-ban-bill). That failure puts at least two proposed projects at risk: a $550 million Sentinel Data Centers facility at the former Jay Paper Mill site and a LiquidCool Solutions project near Limestone.

Governor Mills had publicly supported an exemption for the Jay project, citing its potential to bring jobs and tax revenue to rural western Maine, but the legislature rejected that approach. Mills has not yet indicated whether she will sign, veto, or allow the bill to become law without her signature.

The Data Center Coalition, an industry trade group, warned that the moratorium would "discourage further investment" and "send a signal that the state is closed for business," according to [CNN](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/12/climate/maine-data-center-ban-bill). Similar proposals are under consideration in New York, South Carolina, Oklahoma, and Vermont.

### Nationwide Construction Bottleneck

Maine's legislative action coincides with a broader slowdown in US data center construction. Of the estimated 12 to 16 gigawatts of data center capacity planned for deployment in 2026, only about 5 GW is currently under construction, according to [TechRadar](https://www.techradar.com/pro/if-one-piece-of-your-supply-chain-is-delayed-then-your-whole-project-cant-deliver-nearly-half-of-us-data-centers-planned-for-2026-canceled-or-delayed-and-things-could-soon-get-much-worse). This shortfall persists despite the largest technology companies being expected to spend more than $650 billion on AI infrastructure expansion this year, as reported by [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/half-of-planned-us-data-center-builds-have-been-delayed-or-canceled-growth-limited-by-shortages-of-power-infrastructure-and-parts-from-china-the-ai-build-out-flips-the-breakers).

The primary bottleneck is not computing hardware but electrical infrastructure. Transformers, switchgear, and batteries account for less than 10 percent of total data center construction costs yet have become the binding constraint, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/half-of-planned-us-data-center-builds-have-been-delayed-or-canceled-growth-limited-by-shortages-of-power-infrastructure-and-parts-from-china-the-ai-build-out-flips-the-breakers). Lead times for high-power transformers have stretched from a pre-2020 norm of 24 to 30 months to as long as five years, far exceeding the under-18-month deployment cycles that hyperscale operators target for AI data centers.

US dependence on Chinese electrical components compounds the problem. Imports of high-power transformers from China rose from fewer than 1,500 units in 2022 to more than 8,000 units through October 2025, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/half-of-planned-us-data-center-builds-have-been-delayed-or-canceled-growth-limited-by-shortages-of-power-infrastructure-and-parts-from-china-the-ai-build-out-flips-the-breakers). China also accounts for more than 40 percent of US battery imports and approximately 30 percent of certain transformer and switchgear categories. Tariffs and national security concerns have not yet dislodged this dependency, with Canada, Mexico, and South Korea serving as alternative but insufficient sources.

## What We Don't Know

- Whether Governor Mills will sign LD 307 as passed or push for amendments to exempt projects like the Jay Paper Mill facility.
- How the roughly one dozen other states considering moratorium legislation will respond to Maine setting a precedent.
- Whether the supply chain constraints affecting transformers and switchgear will ease in time for the data centers currently under construction to meet their 2026 delivery targets.
- How the gap between planned and deliverable capacity will affect AI model training timelines and cloud pricing for enterprise customers.

## Analysis

Maine's moratorium, even if modest in direct impact on the national data center market, carries outsized symbolic weight. The state has relatively little hyperscale infrastructure compared to northern Virginia, Texas, or the Pacific Northwest. But by becoming the first to pass a statewide ban, Maine provides a legislative template and political proof of concept for states where far more capacity is at stake.

The convergence of political opposition and physical supply constraints creates a compounding problem for the AI buildout. Even where communities welcome data centers, developers cannot secure the electrical equipment needed to bring facilities online. And even where equipment is available, local opposition has blocked or delayed more than $60 billion in data center investments within a single year, according to [CNN](https://edition.cnn.com/2026/04/12/climate/maine-data-center-ban-bill). The result is a widening gap between the capital committed to AI infrastructure and the actual compute capacity being delivered.
